en.extract:
The Choco sirystes (Sirystes albogriseus), formerly known as the western sirystes,  is a species of passerine bird in the tyrant flycatcher family Tyrannidae. It is found Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama. 


Taxonomy and systematics
The Choco sirystes was formally described in 1863 by the American amateur ornithologist George Newbold Lawrence under the binomial name Lipaugus albogriseus, mistakenly placing it with the cotingas. Lawrence did not mention a type locality but Osbert Salvin and Frederick DuCane Godman, in their book Biologia Centrali-Americana, reported that the specimen had come from "Lion Hill". This was a railway station in the Panama Canal Zone that was submerged when the Gatun Lake was created. It was later moved into genus Sirystes that was introduced in 1860 by the German ornithologists Jean Cabanis and Ferdinand Heine.
By the 1930s what is now the Choco sirystes was generally treated as a subspecies of S. sibilator, at that time called simply "sirystes" and now called the sibilant sirystes. By the beginning of the twenty-first century it was again treated by some authors as a full species, the "western sirystes".  As a result of a study published in 2013, "sirystes" was split into four species, one of which is Sirystes albogriseus which received its current English name Choco sirystes.
The Choco sirystes' specific epithet combines the Latin albus ("white") with the Medieval Latin griseum meaning "gray".
The Choco sirystes is monotypic.


Description
The Choco sirystes is 17.5 to 19 cm (6.9 to 7.5 in) long and weighs 32 to 36 g (1.1 to 1.3 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black crown with a slight crest. The rest of their face is slate gray. Their nape and back are pale gray, their rump white, and their uppertail coverts dark with white fringes. Their wings are dusky black with wide white edges on the coverts and secondaries. Their tail is dusky with grayish white tips on the feathers. Their throat and upper breast are grayish white that becomes white on the rest of the underparts. They have a dark brown iris, a black bill whose base is sometimes paler, and black or dark gray legs and feet. Juveniles are similar to adults with a faint buffy overall tinge.


Distribution and habitat
The Choco sirystes is found from the Panama Canal Zone and Panamá Province east and south through western Colombia into northwestern Ecuador as far as Los Ríos Province. It mostly inhabits humid primary and mature secondary forests in the tropical and lower subtropical zones, where it usually is found in the canopy but comes lower at the forest edge. In elevation it reaches 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in Panama, 600 m (2,000 ft) in Colombia, and 500 m (1,600 ft) in Ecuador.


Behavior


Movement
The Choco sirystes is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The Choco sirystes' diet is not known in detail but includes a variety of insects and also seeds. It often joins mixed-species feeding flocks, taking prey and fruit with sallies from a perch.


Breeding
The Choco sirystes' breeding season is not known but appears to include February. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
The Choco sirystes' most frequent call is "a husky shup-chip-chip or prup-prip-prip-prip". When it is excited it gives "a much faster che-che-che-che-che-che-chut".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Choco sirystes as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its estimated population of between 20,000 and 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered uncommon in Panama and Colombia and "uncommon and local" in Ecuador. 


References


Further reading
Donegan, Thomas (December 2013). "Proposal (#610) to South American Classification Committee – Split Sirystes into two (A) or four (B) species". Retrieved July 17, 2025.

The '''Choco sirystes''' ('''''Sirystes albogriseus'''''), formerly known as the '''western sirystes''',  is a species of passerine bird in the tyrant flycatcher family [[Tyrannidae]]. It is found Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref> 

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Choco sirystes was [[species description|formally described]] in 1863 by the American amateur ornithologist [[George Newbold Lawrence]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Lipaugus albogriseus'', mistakenly placing it with the [[cotinga]]s.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Lawrence | first=George Newbold | author-link=George Newbold Lawrence | date=1867 | publication-date=1863 | title=Catalogue of a collection of birds, made in New Granada, by James McLeannan, Esq., of New York, with notes and descriptions of new species. Part IV | journal=Annals of the Lyceum of Natural History of New York | volume=8 | pages=1-46 [9] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16022238 }}</ref> Lawrence did not mention a [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] but [[Osbert Salvin]] and [[Frederick DuCane Godman]], in their book ''Biologia Centrali-Americana'', reported that the specimen had come from "Lion Hill".<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Salvin | first1=Osbert | author1-link=Osbert Salvin | last2=Godman | first2=Frederick DuCane | author2-link=Frederick DuCane Godman | year=1888–1904 | title=Biologia Centrali-Americana: Aves | volume=2 | location=London | publisher=R.H. Porter | page=47 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37639189 }}</ref> This was a railway station in the [[Panama Canal Zone]] that was submerged when the [[Gatun Lake]] was created.<ref>{{ cite journal | author=Smithsonian Institution  | date=1912 | title=Smithsonian Biological Survey of the Panama Canal Zone | journal=Smithsonian Miscellaneous Collections | volume=59 | issue=11 | pages=15-26 [20, Figure 21] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8814794 }}</ref> It was later moved into [[genus]] ''[[Sirystes]]'' that was introduced in 1860 by the German ornithologists [[Jean Cabanis]] and [[Ferdinand Heine]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

By the 1930s what is now the Choco sirystes was generally treated as a subspecies of ''S. sibilator'', at that time called simply "sirystes" and now called the [[sibilant sirystes]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved 30 March 2025</ref> By the beginning of the twenty-first century it was again treated by some authors as a full species, the "western sirystes".<ref name=Ridgely2>{{cite book | last =Ridgely | first =Robert S. | last2 =Greenfield | first2 =Paul J. | title =The Birds of Ecuador: Field Guide | publisher =Cornell University Press | volume = II| date =2001 | location =Ithaca | page =521 | isbn =978-0-8014-8721-7 }}</ref>  As a result of a study published in 2013, "sirystes" was split into four species, one of which is ''Sirystes albogriseus'' which received its current English name Choco sirystes.<ref name=Donegan>{{cite journal |last=Donegan |first=Thomas |date=2013 |title=Vocal variation and species limits in the genus ''Sirystes'' (Tyrannidae) |url=https://www.proaves.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/Sirystes-Conservacion-Colombiana-19-11-30_1.pdf |journal=Conservacion Colombiana |volume=19 |pages=11–30 |language=English, Spanish |access-date=July 16, 2025 }}</ref><ref name=SACClist/><ref name=IOC15.1/>

The Choco sirystes' [[specific epithet]] combines the [[Latin]] ''albus'' ("white") with the [[Medieval Latin]] ''griseum'' meaning "gray".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=albogriseus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=albogriseus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=28 March 2025 }}</ref>

The Choco sirystes is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The Choco sirystes is {{convert|17.5|to|19|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|32|to|36|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black crown with a slight crest. The rest of their face is slate gray. Their nape and back are pale gray, their rump white, and their uppertail [[coverts]] dark with white fringes. Their wings are dusky black with wide white edges on the coverts and [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]]. Their tail is dusky with grayish white tips on the feathers. Their throat and upper breast are grayish white that becomes white on the rest of the underparts. They have a dark brown iris, a black bill whose base is sometimes paler, and black or dark gray legs and feet. Juveniles are similar to adults with a faint buffy overall tinge.<ref name=CHSI-BOW>del Hoyo, J., N. Collar, and G. M. Kirwan (2020). Choco Sirystes (''Sirystes albogriseus''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.siryst3.01 retrieved July 178, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Choco sirystes is found from the [[Panama Canal Zone]] and [[Panamá Province]] east and south through western Colombia into northwestern Ecuador as far as [[Los Ríos Province]].<ref name=CHSI-BOW/><ref name=Ridgely2/> It mostly inhabits humid [[Primary forest|primary]] and mature [[Secondary forest|secondary]] forests in the tropical and lower subtropical zones, where it usually is found in the canopy but comes lower at the forest edge.<ref name=CHSI-BOW/><ref name=57Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Kevin J. Burns, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-seventh supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2016, vol. 133:544-560 retrieved February 12, 2023</ref> In elevation it reaches {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Panama, {{convert|600|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Colombia, and {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Ecuador.<ref name=vanPerlo2>{{cite book | last =vanPerlo | first =Ber |  title =Birds of Mexico and Central America | publisher =Princeton University Press | series =Princeton Illustrated Checklists | date =2006 | location =New Jersey | pages =Plate 67, map 67.8 | isbn =0-691-12070-6 }}</ref><ref name=McMullan>{{cite book | last1 =McMullan | first1 =Miles | last2 =Donegan | first2 =Thomas M. | last3 =Quevedo | first3 =Alonso |  title = Field Guide to the Birds of Colombia | publisher = Fundación ProAves| date =2010 | location =Bogotá | page =165  |isbn =978-0-9827615-0-2 }}</ref><ref name=Ridgely2/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Choco sirystes is a year-round resident.<ref name=CHSI-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Choco sirystes' diet is not known in detail but includes a variety of insects and also seeds. It often joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s, taking prey and fruit with sallies from a perch.<ref name=CHSI-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Choco sirystes' breeding season is not known but appears to include February. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=CHSI-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Sirystes-albogriseus |species=the Choco sirystes}}
===Vocalization===

The Choco sirystes' most frequent call is "a husky ''shup-chip-chip'' or ''prup-prip-prip-prip''". When it is excited it gives "a much faster ''che-che-che-che-che-che-chut''".<ref name=Ridgely2/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Choco sirystes as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its estimated population of between 20,000 and 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered uncommon in Panama and Colombia and "uncommon and local" in Ecuador.<ref name=vanPerlo2/><ref name=McMullan/><ref name=Ridgely2/>
